title: MiFID II Algo Trading — Message Rate Throttle Breaches vs Exchange Limits

compliance.assurance_rationale: Auto-migrated from cat-22 markdown on Phase 1.3 run, then normalised in Phase A (clause 'unspecified' → 'Art.17'). Requires SME review to confirm clause accuracy, mode (satisfies vs detects-violation-of), and assurance level (contributing/partial/full).

app: Gateway logs (FIX, binary protocol) via HEC
spl:
index=trading sourcetype="gw:rate" breach_flag=true earliest=-30d
| stats count as breaches, perc95(orders_per_sec) as p95_ops by venue, session_id
| lookup algo_session_map.csv session_id OUTPUT algo_id, desk
| sort - breaches
description: Throttle breaches can indicate malfunctioning algos or reckless co-location usage. Continuous comparison of outbound order rates to venue-specific caps evidences real-time surveillance aligned with Art. 17 controls.
value: Throttle breaches can indicate malfunctioning algos or reckless co-location usage. Continuous comparison of outbound order rates to venue-specific caps evidences real-time surveillance aligned with Art. 17 controls.
grandmaExplanation: Throttle breaches can indicate malfunctioning algos or reckless co-location usage — so you can show exactly what is being watched and when something went wrong.
implementation: (1) Maintain `venue_limit_ops` from exchange rule files; (2) map sessions to `algo_id`; (3) auto-throttle at gateway where possible—Splunk is detective; (4) alert desk head; (5) weekly summary to market conduct.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Gateway logs (FIX, binary protocol) via HEC.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=trading` `sourcetype="gw:rate"` (session_id, venue, orders_per_sec, venue_limit_ops, breach_flag, _time).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Maintain `venue_limit_ops` from exchange rule files; (2) map sessions to `algo_id`; (3) auto-throttle at gateway where possible—Splunk is detective; (4) alert desk head; (5) weekly summary to market conduct.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=trading sourcetype="gw:rate" breach_flag=true earliest=-30d
| stats count as breaches, perc95(orders_per_sec) as p95_ops by venue, session_id
| lookup algo_session_map.csv session_id OUTPUT algo_id, desk
| sort - breaches
```

Understanding this SPL

**MiFID II Algo Trading — Message Rate Throttle Breaches vs Exchange Limits** — Throttle breaches can indicate malfunctioning algos or reckless co-location usage. Continuous comparison of outbound order rates to venue-specific caps evidences real-time surveillance aligned with Art. 17 controls.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=trading` `sourcetype="gw:rate"` (session_id, venue, orders_per_sec, venue_limit_ops, breach_flag, _time). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Gateway logs (FIX, binary protocol) via HEC. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: trading; **sourcetype**: gw:rate. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=trading, sourcetype="gw:rate", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by venue, session_id** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (sessions), Bar by venue, Single value (distinct sessions breaching).
visualization: Table (sessions), Bar by venue, Single value (distinct sessions breaching).
knownFalsePositives: Planned maintenance, backups, or batch jobs can drive metrics outside normal bands — correlate with change management windows.
